You are the OPEN-SIDE integrator for the tamarin-rs sealed relicensing campaign, working in /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs. You are open-side: you may read everything, including ported code and the cleanroom. Read first: the graphdot/unit-B sections of /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/INTEGRATION_REPORT.md (rounds 7-9 blockers and history), /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/workspace/INTERFACE.md (the new CellWidths adapter contract from sealed round 10), the round-10 parts of graphdot/workspace/BEHAVIOR.md (§3f), and PROTOCOL.md ("Full-corpus gates").
CONTEXT: sealed round 10 corrected the fill-width laws (census wrapping 81.6%→86.3%, probe byte-exactness 525/547) and added the override interface generate::CellWidths{occupancy,bonus,fill_width} + group_widths_with + RawRule::premise_widths/conclusion_widths — designed for YOU: ~90% of remaining wrap disagreements sit in abbreviated groups whose true widths are computed by the reference on UN-abbreviated internal term renderings, which only the open side can supply.
RULES: sealed sources re-sync is mechanical only (documented recipe; headerless; reverse-transform byte-identity verified). Adapters you write are open-side and only translate/compute inputs — never transplant render logic out of clean files. A clean file acquiring a GPL header is a provenance violation — stop and report. No git commits. PATH needs /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin. Gate scripts carry OOM guards — preserve them. Live HS servers only on ports 3200-3299; stop everything you start.
STEPS:
1. Re-sync the round-10 graph-clean sources from /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/graphdot/workspace/graph-clean/src into the vendored clean graph module in crates/tamarin-server (find its current location and the documented recipe in INTEGRATION_REPORT.md; verify byte-identity by reverse transform).
2. Write the occupancy adapter: compute per-cell CellWidths from the UN-abbreviated term renderings available in the ported pipeline (you may read the ported measurement code — you are open-side), and supply them via RawRule::premise_widths/conclusion_widths at the existing graph_clean call boundary.
3. Measure with the adapter ON: the GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS gate (12,022 captured DOT payloads) — report exact byte-match counts overall and on the wrap-sensitive subset, vs the round-9-era baseline without occupancies. Characterize every remaining divergence family with counts and a first-diverging-byte example (these become the sealed round-11 targets).
4. IF a byte-green adoption surface exists (full or a precisely-characterized subset — e.g. all graphs whose cells carry supplied occupancies): route the live web DOT serialization through the clean module, then gate in order: cargo build --release; full workspace cargo test; live HS spot probes (ports 3200-3299) on at least 3 theories comparing served graph payloads; bash scripts/web_parity.sh; scripts/gen_license_headers.py --check (0 stale, no clean file headered). Delete ported serialization paths ONLY if every caller is byte-green through the clean route; partial adoption is fine; otherwise keep-and-report.
5. Append a dated graph round-10-integration section to INTEGRATION_REPORT.md with exact numbers. Return a plain-text summary: adapter design in two sentences, gate numbers before/after occupancies, adoption outcome, and the ranked residual families for round 11.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Integrator: dirty-room (adapters only; no logic transplanted 4 from replaced files into clean code). Repo: `/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs`. 5 6 Precedent followed: the wellformedness cluster (`crates/tamarin-parser/src/wf/`) 7 — clean sources vendored as an in-crate module with mechanical path fixes plus 8 a small workspace-authored adapter; clean files carry no license header, kept 9 ported files keep theirs. 10 11 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 12 ## 0. Vendoring (both clusters) — DONE 13 14 Clean deliverables copied verbatim into the server crate as in-crate modules, 15 with the single mechanical fix `crate::` -> `super::` (module path re-rooting) 16 and — in `graph_clean/mod.rs` only — the module-doc doctest fence changed 17 ```` ``` ```` -> ```` ```ignore ```` (it referenced `graph_clean::…` as an 18 extern crate, which does not exist once vendored; the doctest is the clean 19 crate's own, not a workspace test). No license headers added — these are the 20 relicensable clean sources. 21 22 * `crates/tamarin-server/src/graph_clean/` <- `graph-clean/src/` 23 (`mod.rs` <- `lib.rs`, `model.rs`, `term.rs`, `abbrev.rs`, `dot.rs`) — 837 LOC. 24 * `crates/tamarin-server/src/web_clean/` <- `web-clean/src/` 25 (`mod.rs` <- `lib.rs`, `envelope`, `errors`, `escape`, `forms`, `intdot`, 26 `page`, `proofscript`, `route`, `text`, `notfound_template`, 27 `shell_template`) — 1005 LOC. 28 * Wired `pub mod graph_clean; pub mod web_clean;` into `lib.rs` (headered file; 29 only the two `pub mod` lines + an explanatory comment were added — the kept 30 file's own header was not touched). 31 32 Fidelity check: `sed 's/super::/crate::/' ` diffs byte-identical to 33 each original clean source (and `mod.rs` modulo the doctest fence). The clean 34 crates' own suites still pass at their workspace locations: `graph-clean` 16 35 round-trip + 7 abbrev + 1 doctest; `web-clean` parity suite. The vendored 36 `web_clean` inline unit tests (19) run and pass inside `tamarin-server`. 37 38 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 39 ## 1. WEB cluster — REWIRED (partial, byte-safe) 40 41 Adapters route prover-generated content as opaque pane/body strings into the 42 clean templates. All routed paths are byte-identical to the ported output for 43 the corpus/fixture theories, or a strictly-more-HS-faithful byte change that 44 the (structural) parity suite accepts. 45 46 REPLACED (ported render logic removed, now sourced from the clean layer): 47 48 * `handlers/theory.rs::intdot` — the ported `intdotLayout` `format!` template 49 deleted; now `web_clean::intdot::render_intdot(&html_escape(name), &dotsrc)`. 50 Byte-identical (the `` prefix `Theory: ` has no escapable chars, so 51 escaping `Theory: NAME` vs escaping `NAME` coincide). 52 * `handlers/theory_html.rs::overview_page` — for **local-origin** theories the 53 whole page shell now comes from `web_clean::page::render_page`, with the 54 west (proof-script state) and center (main-view HTML) panes passed as opaque 55 strings (`"{pane} "` — the trailing space the ported template emitted). Byte- 56 identical to the ported local output (verified head/nav/pane/tail against 57 `shell_template`). 58 * `handlers/theory_html.rs` add/delete forms — `add_lemma_html` and 59 `delete_lemma_html` deleted; `path_html` now calls 60 `web_clean::forms::add_form` / `delete_form`. Byte change: the clean forms 61 reproduce HS's stray `</span>` in the `<noscript>` block (byte-exact HS), 62 which the ported code had normalized away. Structural suite unaffected. 63 64 REMAINS PORTED (kept, headers untouched) — with the precise blocker: 65 66 * `overview_page` **non-local** branch: `web_clean::page`'s shell bakes in the 67 local-origin header (Reload-file / Append-modified-lemmas actions); a 68 non-local (uploaded) origin gates those off, which the clean shell does not 69 model. Routing it would add the two actions — a behavior change — so the 70 ported template is kept for that branch only. 71 * `edit_lemma_html`: `web_clean::forms::edit_form` hard-codes the textarea 72 `rows="8"`; HS sizes it dynamically (`textHeight = 2 + #newlines` in the 73 lemma plaintext). Routing Edit through the clean form would drop that. Gap in 74 `web_clean::forms` (a fixed `rows` slot); kept ported. 75 * `proof_state` (west pane) and the `path_html` main-view bodies (help / 76 message / rules / sources / proof / tactic) stay in the workspace: these ARE 77 the opaque prover content the clean shell consumes — the prover pretty-prints 78 (via `tamarin_theory`) are data the adapter supplies, not scaffolding. 79 `theory_html.rs` is therefore NOT deleted; it is now the pane/body supplier + 80 the two kept branches above. 81 * JSON envelopes (`handlers/json_resp.rs`) and the 404 page: `web_clean:: 82 envelope` (`{html,title}` / `{redirect}`) and `web_clean::errors:: 83 render_not_found` are validated and byte-compatible on keys, BUT the ported 84 `json_resp` returns `axum::Json<Value>` across ~40 call sites (and carries a 85 third `{alert}` variant `web_clean` does not model), and `not_found_response` 86 is a pervasive minimal-body stub with no request-path in scope. Rewiring 87 either is pure return-type/plumbing churn with no behavior win under the 88 structural gate; left for a follow-up (noted, not forced). 89 90 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 91 ## 2. GRAPH/DOT cluster — VENDORED; serializer rewire BLOCKED, reported 92 93 `graph_clean` is vendored, builds, and passes its round-trip corpus. The DOT 94 **pipeline rewire** (`handlers/dot.rs` `system_to_dot` / `system_to_dot_with` 95 / `render_svg_or_dot_with` -> repr/simplify -> adapter -> graph_clean model -> 96 graph_clean abbrev -> graph_clean serialization) is **not** performed: it cannot 97 be a thin adapter without a behavior change, blocked by three concrete gaps 98 between `graph_clean`'s model and the payload the ported serializer produces. 99 Per protocol ("where the clean crate lacks a behavior, leave the old path in 100 place and report the gap; never patch clean code with old-code logic"), the 101 ported `handlers/dot.rs` and `graph/abbreviation.rs` are kept intact (headers 102 untouched). `graph/abbreviation.rs` is therefore NOT deleted. 103 104 Blockers (each independently fatal to a byte-faithful, thin-adapter rewire): 105 106 1. **Term rendering / abbreviation are one coupled behavior the clean crate 107 deliberately omits.** `graph_clean` treats record-cell text as pre-rendered 108 (BEHAVIOR.md §3a `[GAP]`: full solver term pretty-printer, incl. the 109 `renderRow`/`renderBalanced` width-proportional record-row wrapping, is out 110 of scope), and its `abbrev` names/substitutes over its OWN independent 111 `term::Term` model. The ported cell text is rendered from `LNTerm` with the 112 HS-faithful balanced wrapping AND abbreviations substituted in-place 113 (`apply_abbreviations_fact`, `LNTerm`-keyed). `graph_clean::abbrev` cannot 114 substitute a chosen name into `LNTerm`-rendered text; using it for selection 115 would force routing all term rendering through `graph_clean::Term`, losing 116 the balanced wrapping — exactly the behavior the protocol says to keep. So 117 both the cell renderer and `graph/abbreviation.rs` must stay ported. 118 119 2. **No representation for missing-node shapes.** `graph_clean::model::NodeKind` 120 is `Record | Ellipse | Plain`, and `Ellipse` emits `shape="ellipse"` only. 121 HS "missing" nodes are `trapezium` / `invtrapezium` (`dotConcC`/`dotPremC`). 122 A constraint system whose edges reference an absent node cannot be modeled; 123 extending the model would be patching clean code (forbidden). 124 125 3. **HS node/port-id allocation is not modeled.** `graph_clean` takes node ids 126 and record ports verbatim (its round-trip builds them from captured DOT); 127 it does not generate HS's graph-global `<n0>,<n1>,…` port scheme (`Text.Dot` 128 `cacheState`/`dsPrems`/`dsConcs`). Reproducing byte-exact HS ids requires 129 that allocation logic, which lives in the ported serializer being "deleted" 130 — writing it into the adapter would transplant replaced logic. 131 132 Consequence: a rewire that kept the ported cell renderer + abbreviation + 133 missing-node fallback + id scheme would GROW `handlers/dot.rs` (adapter on top 134 of a still-live serializer) rather than thin it, and would not be byte-faithful 135 — the opposite of the task's goal. Recommended: a `graph_clean` round-2 that 136 (a) adds arbitrary `shape=` support (trapezium), (b) exposes an HS port/node-id 137 allocator, and (c) either a `LNTerm`->`Term` bridge or an accepted term-render 138 GAP; then the thin-adapter rewire becomes possible. One isolated sub-component 139 IS cleanly routable today — the legend table via `graph_clean::abbrev:: 140 legend_html` (byte-exact, incl. the 65-space hang indent the ported legend 141 omits) — but wiring only it into the otherwise-kept ported serializer mixes two 142 escaping/whitespace regimes for no net structural gain, so it is left for the 143 round-2 rewire. 144 145 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 146 ## 3. LOC delta 147 148 * Added (vendored clean sources, headerless): `graph_clean` 837 + `web_clean` 149 1005 = **1842 LOC**. 150 * Adapter glue authored in kept files (headerless additions within headered 151 files): ~35 LOC (theory.rs intdot call, overview_page local branch, 152 path_html arms, lib.rs module decls). 153 * Deleted (GPL-headered ported render logic): `add_lemma_html` (~22) + 154 `delete_lemma_html` (~21) + ported `intdot` `format!` template (~15) ≈ **58 155 LOC**. Tracked-file diff: +82 / -70 across `theory.rs`, `theory_html.rs`, 156 `lib.rs`. 157 * Not deleted (blocked/kept, reported above): `handlers/dot.rs` (2266), 158 `graph/abbreviation.rs` (whole), `theory_html.rs` non-local + edit + pane 159 suppliers. 160 161 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 162 ## 4. Validation (all green) 163 164 * `cargo build --workspace` — 0 errors. 165 * `cargo test -p tamarin-server` — lib 80 (incl. 19 vendored `web_clean`), 166 routes_autoprove 6, routes_basic 19, routes_graph 4, routes_proof_step 3, 167 routes_static 3, routes_stubs 15, routes_upload 3; doctest 1 ignored. 0 168 failures. The captured-HS-response parity fixtures (routes_basic JSON-key + 169 structural, routes_stubs) stay green. 170 * `cargo test -p tamarin-parser` — all green (67 + 2). 171 * Graph fixture tests (`graph-clean` crate): 16 round-trip + 7 abbrev + 1 172 doctest, 0 failures. Vendored copy verified byte-identical modulo path fixes. 173 174 ================================================================================ 175 # Dirty-room integration report — units C, D, E, F, G 176 177 Date: 2026-07-17. Integrator: dirty-room (adapters only; no logic transplanted 178 from replaced files into clean code). Same protocol as above. 179 180 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 181 ## C. Wellformedness round-3 — RE-SYNCED (part 1) + KEEP-AND-REPORT (part 2) 182 183 ### C.1 Re-sync of clean sources into `crates/tamarin-parser/src/wf/` — DONE 184 Re-applied the established mechanical path-fix recipe (`crate::{pretty,report, 185 formula,checks}` -> `super::…`; `crate::ast` kept, it resolves to the real 186 tamarin-parser AST) to the round-3 clean sources: 187 188 * `wf/checks.rs` <- wf-clean/src/checks.rs (grew 46.7K->61.2K: adds 189 `fact_capitalization`, `formula_terms_reducible`/`formula_terms`, the reducible 190 de-Bruijn term renderer, the round-3 guardedness two modes + wrapped printer). 191 * `wf/formula.rs` <- wf-clean/src/formula.rs (adds `pp_formula_wrapped`, the 192 HughesPJ-style multi-line layout engine). 193 * `wf/pretty.rs`, `wf/report.rs` <- byte-identical clean sources (only 194 `crate::ast`, kept). 195 * `wf/mod.rs` rebuilt from wf-clean/src/lib.rs with the two transforms 196 (`pub mod ast;` dropped; `pub use ast::*;` -> `pub use crate::ast::*;`) and the 197 PRESERVED workspace lines `pub mod order; pub use order::*;` re-appended. 198 `wf/order.rs` untouched (workspace-authored). 199 200 Validation: `cargo test -p tamarin-parser` green (67 + 2 wellformedness); oracle 201 harness `wellformedness_fixtures` = 21/21 parsed / Rust-wf-match / Tamarin-match 202 (100%). Downstream `cargo build -p tamarin-theory -p tamarin-server` clean. 203 204 ### C.2 check_terms.rs / check_guarded_wf residue — KEPT, gaps reported 205 The task's target rewire (route `check_terms::check_terms_wf`'s formula-terms 206 through `wf::formula_terms_reducible`, and the `elaborate::check_guarded_wf` call 207 sites through the clean two-mode guardedness) is NOT performed: both clean 208 entry points have an ORACLE-CONFIRMED behavior gap that a thin adapter cannot 209 close without transplanting the replaced sort-kind logic. Per protocol the 210 ported paths are kept with their GPL-pending headers and the gaps reported. 211 212 Blocker 1 — formula-terms variable binding (PROVEN divergence). The clean 213 `formula_terms_reducible` binds a variable use to a quantifier by NAME ONLY 214 (`checks::debruijn_index`, BEHAVIOR.md "round2 fix"). That is correct for 215 Msg-vs-Untagged and temporal `#i`-vs-`i` collisions but WRONG when a use's 216 sort-KIND differs from a same-named binder. Direct oracle probe (HS v1.13.0 217 binary, wf_oracle.sh) on 218 lemma L: "All #x. (K(x) @ #x) ==> F" 219 prints `Lemma `L' uses terms of the wrong form: `Free x'` 220 because the node binder `#x` does NOT bind the message-position use `x`. The 221 clean name-only model binds it (`Bound 0`) and reports NO offender — a silent 222 regression. The ported `check_terms.rs` matches by name AND sort-kind AND idx 223 (`lookup_bound`/`kind_of`) and reproduces the oracle byte-exactly; its unit test 224 `untagged_message_use_does_not_bind_to_node_binder` is the captured case and 225 stays green. The gap is intrinsic to the clean detection algorithm (baked into 226 `debruijn_index`), so it cannot be split out into an adapter. 227 228 Blocker 2 — guardedness fidelity. The clean `formula_guardedness` is an admitted 229 HEURISTIC over-approximation (BEHAVIOR.md "Guardedness ALGORITHM depth: ∃ failure 230 sub-modes and exotic ∀ bodies beyond the probed cases" is a listed gap; guard 231 variables also matched by NAME only), and it only iterates LEMMAS. The ported 232 `elaborate::check_guarded_wf` drives the REAL semantic conversion 233 `formula_to_guarded` (the solver machinery, which stays ported regardless) and 234 renders via the ported `pretty_formula`; it is byte-exact in the run pipeline. 235 Routing the run.rs/theory_io.rs call sites through the documented-incomplete 236 heuristic risks exactly the captured-HS byte-parity the task requires to stay 237 green, with no faithful fallback available. (Restriction guardedness is not a 238 real divergence either way: an unguarded restriction is a FATAL HS error at 239 Guarded.hs:526, verified by oracle, so it never reaches the wf report.) 240 241 Consequence: `check_terms.rs` is NOT deleted; `check_guarded_wf` and its two call 242 sites (`run.rs`, `theory_io.rs`) are unchanged. The round-3 clean checks DO ship 243 in the parser-level `wf::check_theory` (pre-elaboration, fixture-validated). A 244 future close would need the wf crate to carry the sort-kind binding distinction 245 and a semantic (formula_to_guarded-equivalent) guardedness decision; then the 246 post-elaboration rewire becomes byte-faithful. 247 248 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 249 ## D. Console (cli-clean) — VENDORED + help/version ROUTED; parse/framing KEPT 250 251 Vendored `console/workspace/cli-clean/src` into `crates/tamarin-prover/src/cli/` 252 (suffix-free, headerless) with the mechanical `crate::` -> `super::` fix and the 253 `include_str!` fixture path `../fixtures/` -> `fixtures/` (fixtures copied to 254 `cli/fixtures/`): `modes.rs`, `parse.rs`, `help.rs`, `version.rs`, `framing.rs`, 255 `errors.rs`. The ported `cli.rs` became `cli/mod.rs` (kept, GPL header intact). 256 Workspace adapter authored: `cli/adapt.rs` (headerless). 257 258 ROUTED through the clean layer (ported bodies deleted): 259 * `--help`: `cli/mod.rs::help_text()` body deleted; `adapt::help_text(&sub)` maps 260 the ported `Subcommand` -> clean `Mode` and returns `help::render_help(mode)`. 261 `run.rs` (show_help), `main.rs` (parse-error fallback), and `run.rs`'s batch 262 "no input files" envelope now emit the clean per-mode HS help page. SPOT-CHECK: 263 `tamarin-prover --help` is now BYTE-IDENTICAL to the HS capture 264 (`cli/fixtures/help_global.txt`) — the ported help was a Rust-port-relabeled 265 page ("...verification (Rust port).") that diverged from HS. 266 * `--version`: `version_text()` + `version_maude_stderr_text()` bodies deleted; 267 `adapt::version_stdout()` fills the clean `version.tmpl` slots from this 268 binary's build metadata (`VERSION`/`GIT_REV`/`GIT_BRANCH`/`BUILD_TIMESTAMP` + 269 detected Maude version) and `run.rs` prints it to stdout. SPOT-CHECK: the 270 static template is byte-identical to the HS `--version` capture (dynamic git/ 271 compile/maude slots carry this build's own values); the ported code emitted a 272 DIFFERENT (banner-then-maude, non-interleaved) form. 273 Two ported unit tests (`version_stdout_*` / `version_stderr_*`) that pinned the 274 removed stream-split behavior were deleted; `adapt.rs` carries replacement 275 byte-parity tests against the vendored fixtures. 276 277 KEPT ported (headers intact) — gaps reported: 278 * Arg parsing (`parse_args` + typed `Args`/`Subcommand` + value validation). The 279 clean `parse`/`modes` flag tables model only the HS flag set; this binary adds 280 Rust-specific flags the clean tables do NOT list — `--processors`, 281 `--maude-processes`, `--data-dir` — and the clean `parse` returns an untyped 282 `(name,value)` flag list with no int-parsing / typed defaults / range errors 283 (`bound: invalid bound given`, etc.). Routing tokenization through clean would 284 reject the Rust flags ("Unknown flag") and drop typed validation; per protocol 285 the ported typed parser is kept. (The clean `parse`/`errors` modules are 286 vendored and available for a future pre-strip adapter.) 287 * Batch output framing (`run.rs` maude preamble / `[Theory X]` progress markers / 288 `summary of summaries` block). 1	# graph-clean — adapter-facing width-input interface (round 10)
2	
3	Audience: the OPEN-side integrator wiring an adapter later. Everything here is
4	implemented and test-gated in `workspace/graph-clean`; semantics trace to
5	BEHAVIOR.md §3f (Session 10).
6	
7	## Why this exists
8	
9	The reference decides record-row wrapping (which cells break, and how wide a
10	broken cell's fill is) on the *internal* term widths — notably the
11	UN-abbreviATED renderings — while this crate consumes post-abbreviation display
12	text. The crate's estimates reproduce 96.6 % of corpus cells; the dominant
13	residual is exactly this internal-width family. An adapter that sits on the
14	term representation can close it by supplying the values directly.
15	
16	## Surface
17	
18	```rust
19	pub struct generate::CellWidths {
20	    pub occupancy:  Option<i64>, // row occupancy C (columns this cell counts
21	                                 // for in its SIBLINGS' trigger budgets)
22	    pub bonus:      Option<i64>, // the cell's own trigger-budget bonus
23	    pub fill_width: Option<i64>, // fill numerator (internal width) once the
24	                                 // cell wraps
25	}
26	pub fn generate::group_widths_with(cells: &[String],
27	                                   overrides: &[Option<CellWidths>]) -> Vec<usize>;
28	// RawRule builders (one Option<CellWidths> per premise / conclusion cell):
29	RawRule::premise_widths(Vec<Option<CellWidths>>)
30	RawRule::conclusion_widths(Vec<Option<CellWidths>>)
31	```
32	
33	## Semantics (precise)
34	
35	Per record group (premises, or conclusions; the info cell is its own group):
36	
37	- trigger: cell *i* wraps iff `flat_i > max(87 + bonus_i − Σ_{j≠i} C_j, 20)`
38	  (lone cell: budget exactly 87, no bonus);
39	- fill (only if wrapping): ribbon
40	  `clamp(round(87·N_i / (N_i + Σ_{j≠i} w_j·C_j)), 20, flat_i − 1)`,
41	  `w_j = 5/6` for a single-quoted-atom sibling when cell *i* has a top-level
42	  tuple argument, else 1; the cell is laid out at lineLength `⌊3·ribbon/2⌋`,
43	  ribbonsPerLine 1.5 (ragged HughesPJ fill).
44	
45	Overrides substitute per FIELD: `C_i` ← `occupancy`, `bonus_i` ← `bonus`,
46	`N_i` ← `fill_width`; each absent field (or absent entry, or a short/empty
47	override slice) falls back to the display-text estimate for that field:
48	
49	- `C_i` default: `flat_i + Σ_{top-level tuple/union args}(elems + 1)`;
50	- `bonus_i` default: max over those args of `⌊elems/2⌋ + 2` (an arg with ≥ 9
51	  elements contributes 4); 0 with no such arg;
52	- `N_i` default: `flat_i + Σ_{union args}(elems + 1) + #function-nodes`.
53	
54	The display flat width `flat_i` always comes from the cell text (it IS the
55	rendered content). With every input absent the output is byte-identical to
56	`group_widths` — regression-gated by
57	`tests/generate_tests.rs::supplied_cell_widths_override_estimates` and
58	`::raw_rule_supplied_widths_reach_cells`, plus the corpus census.
59	
60	## What an adapter should pass
61	
62	For each premise/conclusion cell, compute on the UN-abbreviATED internal term:
63	
64	- `occupancy`: the internal flat width (with tuple/union args measured in
65	  their internal form — the display-side law `+ (elems+1)` / spaced `++`
66	  separators approximates exactly this);
67	- `fill_width`: the same internal width used as the cell's fill numerator;
68	- `bonus`: only if the adapter can compute the reference's own-budget bonus;
69	  otherwise leave `None` (the display-text law is probe-exact on
70	  un-abbreviated shapes).
71	
72	Passing only `occupancy` (the biggest lever — it fixes the sibling budgets) is
73	valid; fields are independent.
74

1 # BEHAVIOR.md — observed spec of the tamarin web-UI constraint-system graph payload 2 3 All statements below are derived from OBSERVED oracle output: the 81 captured 4 crawl manifests under `oracle/captured_responses/` (14705 graph payloads) and 5 targeted live probing of the black-box HS binary (see QUERIES.log). No 6 tamarin-prover source was read. Where a behavior could not be pinned down 7 black-box it is marked **[GAP]**. 8 9 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 10 ## 1. Transport / payload family 11 12 A crawl manifest is JSON: `{base, lemmas, log, capped, manifest}` where 13 `manifest` maps a URL to `{kind, status, body}` (kind ∈ html|text|json|dot). 14 15 Graph-bearing URL families (theory index `N` replaces `#` in captures): 16 17 | URL family | kind | payload | 18 |---|---|---| 19 | `/thy/trace/N/interactive-graph-def/proof/[/...]` | `dot` | **the graph** — graphviz DOT text (`text/plain`) | 20 | `/thy/trace/N/intdot/proof/[/...]` | `html` | wrapper page embedding `` | 21 | `/thy/trace/N/main/cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` | `json` | `{html,title}`; html contains `` | 22 23 The DOT text at `interactive-graph-def` is the whole deliverable. `intdot` is a 24 fixed HTML shell (only the `` and the `dotsrc` path vary). The graph is 25 rendered client-side from the DOT by `/static/js/intdot-graph.es.js`. 26 27 **JSON graph payload** [GAP]: the binary exposes a *separate* `--with-json` 28 rendering backend (`tamarin-prover interactive --help`). No JSON rendering tool 29 is installed here and no `interactive-graph-def` body in the corpus is JSON — 30 the entire corpus is the DOT backend. JSON graph format is therefore unobserved. 31 32 **Graph options** [RESOLVED §8]: the graph URL *does* take query parameters, but 33 they are cookie-driven and added by the UI JS — `simplification=N` (level, default 34 2, always sent), plus the flags `uncompact=`/`uncompress=` (sent at level 0), 35 `unabbreviate=`, `no-auto-sources=`, `clustering=true`. The earlier 36 "byte-identical" observation was because varying only `simplification=1/2/3` does 37 not change output on any probed graph (see §8). The real transforms are triggered 38 by the boolean flags. See §8. 39 40 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 41 ## 2. DOT document structure 42 43 Two headers occur, selected by a single rule (§4): 44 45 ### 2a. Simple header (non-clustered) 46 ``` 47 digraph "G" { 48 nodesep="0.3"; 49 ranksep="0.3"; 50 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2"]; 51 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica"]; 52 <statements…> 53 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 ### 2b. Compact header (clustered) 58 ``` 59 digraph "G" { 60 nodesep="0.8"; 61 ranksep="0.8"; 62 sep="4"; 63 splines="true"; 64 overlap="false"; 65 pack="true"; 66 packmode="cluster"; 67 concentrate="true"; 68 compound="true"; 69 remincross="true"; 70 mclimit="10"; 71 nslimit="20"; 72 nslimit1="20"; 73 ordering="out"; 74 rankdir="TB"; 75 showboxes="false"; 76 clusterrank="local"; 77 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2",margin="0.05,0.05",shape="ellipse"]; 78 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",penwidth="1.5",arrowsize="0.5",color="black",style="solid",weight="8"]; 79 <statements…> 80 81 } 82 ``` 83 84 ### Block / whitespace rule (byte-exact) 85 Every block (`digraph … {`, `subgraph … {`, anonymous `{`) is emitted as: 86 `OPEN "\n"` then each inner statement as `STMT "\n"`, then one blank line, then 87 `}`. i.e. the body always ends `…\n\n}`. The top-level digraph is followed by a 88 final `\n`. Subgraph/anonymous blocks are themselves statements of their parent 89 (so the parent supplies the `\n` after their closing `}`). There are **no** blank 90 lines between sections (header attrs / nodes / edges / subgraphs run together); 91 the only blank line in each block is the one before its own `}`. 92 An empty graph is header attrs + blank + `}` (154 bytes). 93 94 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 95 ## 3. Node & edge syntax 96 97 Only three node shapes appear in proof graphs: `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 98 99 ### 3a. Record node (a rule / graph-node instance) 100 ``` 101 n<ID>[shape="record",label="{{<p> cell|<p> cell}|{<p> cell}|{<p> cell|<p> cell}}",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="black|white",role="<Role>"]; 102 ``` 103 - Attribute order is fixed: `shape,label,fillcolor,style,fontcolor,role`. 104 - Label is a graphviz record: three top groups `{prem}|{info}|{concl}` (a group 105 may be absent/empty). Each group = cells joined by `|`. Each cell = 106 `<portid> text` (port id like `n0`, a space, then the fact text). Ports are 107 numbered per-graph; a record's own node id is allocated AFTER its port ids. 108 - Record-label text escaping: `<`→`\<`, `>`→`\>`, literal `{ } |`→`\{ \} \|`; 109 long facts are wrapped with `\l` (left-justified break) and indented with 110 ` ` runs. (The exact wrap width / term pretty-printing is **[GAP]** — it 111 is graphviz-layout / solver-term-renderer dependent; the model treats cell 112 text as pre-rendered.) 113 - `fillcolor` is a deterministic per-rule color (same rule name ⇒ same color 114 within a graph). `role="Undefined"` for plain multiset-rewriting rules; 115 otherwise the process/agent role. `fontcolor` is `black` on the light MSR 116 palette, `white` on the saturated per-role cluster palette. Exact color hash 117 is **[GAP]**; the model carries colors explicitly. 118 119 ### 3b. Ellipse node (atomic / knowledge / action / temporal node) 120 ``` 121 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse"]; 122 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse",color="<c>"]; 123 ``` 124 - Attribute order: `label,shape[,color]`. Optional `color` (`gray` for `!KU` 125 intruder-knowledge nodes, `darkblue` for action/event nodes, `gray30`, …). 126 - Ellipse text is NOT record-escaped (`<`,`>` appear literally, e.g. 127 `Session( 'I', <pk(~ltkA), pkB>, … )`). 128 129 ### 3c. Edge 130 ``` 131 n<S>[:<sport>] -> n<D>[:<dport>][<attrs>]; 132 ``` 133 Endpoints optionally carry a `:port` (record port). `<attrs>` is a 134 comma-separated attribute list, in observed key order. Frequent styles: 135 136 | attrs | meaning (inferred from context) | 137 |---|---| 138 | `color="red",style="dashed"` | intruder-deduction / `!KU` edge | 139 | `style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"` | structural premise/conclusion edge | 140 | `style="bold",weight="10.0"` | structural edge (uncolored) | 141 | `color="gray30"` | message/std edge | 142 | `color="blue3",style="dashed"` | temporal-order edge | 143 | `color="orangered2"` | (deduction variant) | 144 | `color="black",style="dashed"` | before/less-than temporal edge | 145 | `style="invis"` | ranking edge to the legend node | 146 | `color="green",style="dotted"` / `purple`/`darkorange3` dashed | other goal edges | 147 148 ### 3d. Node shapes (§ Round-3 item 2) 149 Corpus census (all 12 022 dot + 81 manifests): only `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 150 **No trapezium appears in the crawl** — because the crawl captured only *proof* 151 graphs (solved states). Live probing (NAXOS_eCK, NSLPK3 case-distinction graphs) 152 adds one more shape: 153 - **`invtrapezium`** — a `(#var, idx)` placeholder for an **unresolved graph node** 154 referenced by a still-open premise, e.g. `n9[label="(#i, 0)",shape="invtrapezium"]`. 155 `#var` is the (temporal) node variable, `idx` the premise index; the node is fed 156 by a structural edge from the conclusion that must satisfy that premise 157 (`n5:n2 -> n9[style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"]`). Appears in both the 158 compressed default and the uncompressed variant, in every source-case graph 159 tested. Attr order = ellipse's (`label,shape`). 160 - **`trapezium`** (spec-named dual: an unresolved *source* node feeding a present 161 premise) was **NOT observed** in any probe (Session-3 NAXOS/NSLPK3 case graphs, 162 Session-5 re-scan of NSLPK3 `cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` for i≤12,j≤8) nor anywhere 163 in the corpus/manifests. The invtrapezium is the *conclusion→absent-node* case 164 (its edge points **into** it: `n5:n2 -> n6`), i.e. the "conclusion feeding a node 165 absent from the graph"; the un-seen dual would be the mirror (`absent -> premise`). 166 Recorded unobserved; `GraphNode::Shaped{label,shape,color}` (generic) can emit any 167 shape a caller supplies. 168 169 ### 3g. Bare-timepoint ellipse + the `#last` designated timepoint (§ Round-5 item 3) 170 An uncolored ellipse whose label is a bare timepoint variable `#<var>`: 171 `n<ID>[label="#<var>",shape="ellipse"];`. Corpus census of ellipse labels found, 172 besides the `!KU(..)@#t` / `#t:rule[..]` / `Action(..)@#t` forms, bare-timepoint 173 nodes `#i` (1328), `#decrypt` (790), `#t0…#t6`, `#j`, `#j.1`, and the **designated 174 last timepoint `#last`** (107, e.g. `24a119958f784d43.dot`). `#last` arises when a 175 constraint system carries a last timepoint (induction / trace-property proofs); it 176 is the target of a `color="black",style="dashed"` before-edge. All render as the 177 plain uncolored ellipse above. Modelled as `GraphNode::Temporal{var}` (with 178 `GraphNode::last()` for `#last`). 179 180 ### 3e. Node-id / port allocation (§ Round-3 item 3) — byte-verified over 12 022 181 One global monotonic counter, ids handed out in **emission order** so file order == 182 id order. For each node in order: 183 - a **record** takes one id per cell (in cell order: premises, then the info cell, 184 then conclusions) and **then** one id for the node itself — so a `k`-cell record 185 occupies `n<p>…n<p+k-1>` (ports) and `n<p+k>` (node); 186 - every other kind (ellipse / plain / invtrapezium) takes exactly one id. 187 188 Example: a 4-prem/1-info/3-concl record takes ports `n0…n6`, node `n7`; the next 189 ellipse is `n8`. Verified 12022/12022 in `mine_ids.py` and again in Rust 190 (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`, via `NodeIdAllocator`). 191 192 ### 3f. Record-cell term rendering + line wrapping (§ Round-3 item 1) 193 **Group structure** (byte-verified over 160 409 records): the label is 194 `{grp}|{grp}|{grp}` where the groups are the *non-empty* ones among 195 `[premises, info, conclusions]`, in that order; the **info** group is always 196 present (100 %). An empty premise or conclusion group is dropped (a source rule 197 renders `{info}|{concl}`). 198 - **Info cell**: `#<temporal> : <RuleName>` and, if the rule has action facts, 199 `[<action>, …]` (e.g. `#i : I_Complete[Complete( … ), …]`, or bare `#vf.5 : Fresh`). 200 - **Premise/conclusion cell**: a single fact. 201 - **Fact spacing**: a fact pads — `Name( a, b )`, and `Name( )` with no args; 202 a **function application** does not — `f(a, b)`; a **tuple** is `<a, b>`. Verified 203 against the corpus. 204 - **Escaping**: inside a record label, `< > { } |` each get a leading backslash; 205 everything else (quotes, `~ $ ^ * ⊕`, spaces) is literal. 206 - **Line wrapping FORMAT** (byte-exact). When a group breaks, physical lines are 207 separated by `\l` (with a trailing `\l`), and each continuation line is indented 208 with a ` ` run **equal to the column of the broken group's first element** 209 (just after `( ` for a fact, after `<` for a tuple, after `[` for an action 210 list). Verified across 188 192 wrapped cells. 211 - **Line wrapping DECISION — RESOLVED (Session 4): a fixed-width paragraph fill, 212 width = 87 columns, measured per fact from column 0.** The Round-3 claim ("first 213 line 5..87, not a flat width, not derivable") is **refuted**: it *is* a flat 214 width. Established by driving crafted single-node theories 215 (`Out(<'a01', …, 'aN'>)`, `Out(<'aa…a', 'y'>)`) through the live server and 216 sweeping the term width one column at a time: 217 - **W = 87 absolute, functor-invariant.** A fact stays on one line iff its flat 218 rendering is ≤ 87 columns; at 88 it breaks. Continuation lines use the 227 §3f indent. Example `Out(<'a01'…'a12'>)` (flat 91): line 0 = 228 `Out( <'a01', … 'a11', ` (eleven elements + trailing `, `, ending col 83), 229 `'a12'>` on the next line, then the fact's `)` on its own line. 230 - **Delimiter peel — BYTE-IMPLEMENTED (Session 5).** Re-probed the boundary with 231 fresh single-node sweeps (`E10..E14`, `W69..W74`, ports 3210): a **tuple's `>`** 232 stays with the last element iff it fits (`Out( <'aaa(72)', 'y'>` │ `)`), else it 233 peels onto its own line at the tuple's **`<` column** (`W74`: `…'y'` │ `<5sp>>` 234 │ `)`); a **fact's `)` ALWAYS** peels onto its own line at **col 0** once the 235 fact wraps — even when it would fit (`E12`: `'a12'>` │ `)`) — because the padded 236 ` )` space is the break. An **info action-list `]`** stays attached to its last 237 action. An unbreakable atom wider than the budget overflows verbatim; only the 238 trailing delimiters wrap. 239 - **Continuation lookahead — RESOLVED (subsumed).** The old "first line 11, 240 continuation 12" claim is a mis-read: the continuation packs **greedily to the 241 same width 87** as the first line (`E13`→2, `E14`→3 elements on the second line, 242 each ending well under 87). There is no ±1 combinator lookahead to reproduce. 243 - **Info action-list = vertical `sep`, not fill.** An overflowing info cell puts 244 **one action per line** (corpus `6738eb64…`: 4 actions → 4 lines, short actions 245 included), `]` on the last; whereas a tuple / fact-arg list uses the greedy 246 fill. Two distinct combinators, both byte-implemented. 247 - **`graph-clean::render::wrap_cell`** implements all of the above (`layout_fact` 248 fill+`)`-peel, `layout_tuple` fill+`>`-peel, `layout_info` vertical `sep`, 249 `run_layout` greedy fill, `split_top_commas`), wired into 250 `generate::build_record`; byte-verified against the 7 captured probe fixtures 251 (`wrap_E11..E14`, `wrap_W71/W72/W74`). 252 - **The wrap TRIGGER is a per-GROUP shared budget — RESOLVED (Session 6).** The 253 old per-cell `flat > 87` decision is wrong for multi-cell groups. Each record 254 group (`premises`, `info`, `conclusions`) is laid out **independently** (a 255 conclusion cell's wrap ignores premise/info widths, and vice versa — live 256 probes PM/R: a lone conclusion `Out` of flat 87 stays flat with premises of 257 flat 69/103/198 and rule names of length 40). Within a group, the cells share 258 a budget: 259 > For a premise/conclusion group, let `T = Σ(flat width of every cell in the 260 > group)`. Cell *i* wraps **iff** `flat_i > budget_i`, where 261 > `budget_i = max(87 − (T − flat_i), 20)`. Equivalently: **cell *i* wraps iff 262 > `T > 87` AND `flat_i > 20`.** 263 Established by controlled live 2-/3-cell sweeps (QUERIES §6): with a fixed 264 second cell of flat *q*, the first cell's fit/wrap boundary is exactly 265 `flat = 87 − q` (q=11 → 76/77, q=28 → 58/61, q=48 → 39/40, q=68 → 19/22); a 266 third cell adds into the same Σ (3-cell [p,28,28] flips at `flat = 87−56 = 31`). 267 The **floor 20** is a per-cell minimum budget (a cell of flat ≤ 20 never wraps; 268 live: Fb flat 98 forces the sibling budget negative yet the sibling fits at 269 flat ≤ 20, wraps at 21). Verified by hand on `ref_raw_1_1` (Wide rule): premise 270 `In` flat 67 does **not** wrap (budget 87−10 = 77) while conclusion `Big` flat 271 68 **does** (budget 87−(25+14) = 48); `Ack` flat 25 wraps (budget 20), `Out(h)` 272 flat 14 fits — all four correct. 273 - **The `#t : Rule[…]` INFO cell** is its own single-cell group (budget 87, 274 independent of prem/concl), with an added rule: **an info cell with ≥ 2 action 275 facts ALWAYS goes vertical** (one action per line), regardless of width — a 276 ≤ 1-action info wraps only when its flat > 87. Corpus census: 0 non-wrapped 277 info cells carry a top-level action-list comma (all ≥2-action infos wrap). 278 - **Corpus validation at scale.** The rule matches actual `\l` on **99.635 %** of 279 776 259 cells and **98.324 %** of 160 409 records (info cells 99.968 %). Every 280 one of the 2 831 residual cells is within ~1–2 columns of its budget (mismatch 281 `T`-distribution clusters entirely at 79 ≤ T ≤ 95). At that exact boundary the 282 outcome is a **±1 HughesPJ `fits`/ribbon rounding artifact** (live: at a group 283 total of exactly `budget+1` the fit/wrap flips with atom parity; at `budget+5` 284 it wraps cleanly), depending on the cell's token structure, order, and the port 285 marker widths — reproducing it byte-exactly needs the GPL pretty-printer's 286 internal `fits`. Two residuals remain **[GAP]**: (a) this ±1 boundary flip, and 287 (b) the exact greedy fill width when a *multi-cell* cell wraps (live probeB: the 288 per-line element count is not a clean function of the budget). The budget 289 **trigger** itself (does a cell wrap) is byte-exact away from the boundary. 290 - **The FILL width once a cell wraps ≠ the trigger budget — REFINED (Session 7).** 291 The wrap *trigger* is the flat-sum budget above (which cell breaks). The *fill* 292 width (how a broken cell packs) is **wider**: a sibling that itself wraps 293 occupies only the width it is ALLOCATED, not its flat width, so the remaining 294 cell packs more elements per line. Live datum (`Wide` conclusion group 295 `[Ack 25, Big 68, Out 11]`): the 68-flat `Big` cell packs **8** tuple elements 296 on line 0, as if its budget were **56** — not the flat-sum `87−(25+11) = 51` 297 (which packs 7). 1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: the FULL-CORPUS gate for the slice must pass, not a
43	   fixture subset (see "Full-corpus gates" below). A narrow fixture suite that
44	   passes while the corpus gate fails is not acceptance.
45	4. Integration: the open side may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
46	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
47	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
48	   with them (history retains them under GPL). Every integrator that touches a
49	   slice with a full-corpus gate MUST run that gate before declaring green.
50	
51	## Full-corpus gates (standard, not optional)
52	
53	Each slice has a fast oracle gate over the whole 419-file example corpus,
54	diffing the slice's output against the Haskell reference cache. These are the
55	sealed implementer's primary data source (run between build and the HS cache
56	every iteration) AND the integrator's mandatory acceptance check. Both take
57	`RS_PATH` (point at any build) and `ALLOWLIST` (subset while iterating).
58	
59	- Wellformedness: `scripts/wf_gate.sh` — runs the binary WITHOUT `--prove`
60	  (the wf block prints at theory-load, ~1s/file; full corpus ~72s at JOBS=6),
61	  extracts the `/* WARNING … */` block, diffs vs `scripts/.hs_file_cache`.
62	- Web: `scripts/web_parity.sh` — crawls HS and RS interactive servers per
63	  theory, semantic-diffs the response manifests vs `scripts/.web_hs_cache`.
64	- Full batch (proof output, slow ~30-60min): `scripts/corpus_file_diff.sh` —
65	  the ground-truth `--prove` byte diff; run at milestones, not every iteration.
66	
67	Once a slice's ported code is DELETED, the integrated module IS the sealed
68	reimplementation, so running the gate against the tree observes the sealed
69	code's own output vs the reference — legitimate feedback, not observation of
70	replaced code. For a not-yet-integrated slice the tree still holds the ported
71	code, so gate only the workspace build (via `RS_PATH`), never the stock tree.
72

================================================================================
# Open-side integration report — graph round-10 (CellWidths occupancy adapter)
#   graph_clean RE-SYNCED to the round-10 layout engine + width-override
#   interface; the occupancy adapter is BUILT and MEASURED on the full
#   GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS gate; it REGRESSES byte-match, so it is NOT adopted and the
#   serialization swap stays KEPT. The corpus numbers re-pin the residual and
#   name a concrete round-11 interface fix.

Date: 2026-07-18. Integrator: open side (adapters + measurement only; no logic
transplanted into clean files; sealed sources re-synced mechanically). Repo:
`/home/kamilner/tamarin-rs`. Rebased on the round-8 vendored graph_clean (the
last unit-B pass) + the current tree. Outcome: **round-10 is RE-SYNCED
(byte-faithful); the occupancy adapter — legend-expansion → un-abbreviated
internal widths → `CellWidths` overrides — is built and run over all 12 022 DOT
payloads. Supplying the internal widths REGRESSES corpus byte-match (wrapping
cells 86.26 % → 83.89 %; per-cell it FIXES 372 and BREAKS 5 635, net −5 263), so
it is NOT adopted; the live DOT serialization stays on the byte-faithful ported
`handlers/dot::render_balanced`.** No headered file added or deleted (133 → 133).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## B.0 Re-sync graph_clean <- round-10 workspace — DONE (clean, headerless)

`crates/tamarin-server/src/graph_clean/` <- graphdot round-10 workspace
(`graph-clean/src/`). Only THREE files changed vs the round-8 vendored copy:
`doclayout.rs`, `generate.rs`, `pretty.rs` (the round-9/10 fill-engine + width
laws: union/function-application cell documents, the size-corrected flat-sum
`C = flat + Σ(elems+1)` trigger with `⌊n/2⌋+2` bonus, the internal-numerator
proportional fill, and the NEW override surface). The other eight
(`abbrev,alloc,dot,model,options,render,term` + `mod`) are byte-stable after the
established `crate::` → `super::` transform (`alloc/options/render` differ from a
naive reverse only in the pre-existing test-module `use super::*;`; `mod.rs` =
`lib.rs` modulo the one ` ```ignore ` doctest fence). Mechanical `crate::` →
`super::` only; forward transform verified byte-exact (reverse-transform of each
re-synced file diffs the workspace source with zero content lines — only the
`use super::*;` test artifact). Tripwire: `gen_license_headers.py` adds NO header
— all three re-synced files start with `//!` doc comments and carry no derivation
citation the scanner keys on (`pretty.rs`'s `Annotated/HughesPJ.hs` names the
BSD `pretty` library, `generate.rs`'s "the GPL solver" is prose — both were
already headerless in round 8).

The NEW round-10 override surface now lives in the vendored module:
`generate::CellWidths { occupancy, bonus, fill_width }`, `group_widths_with`,
`RawRule::premise_widths / conclusion_widths`. Gates: workspace graph-clean
suite 22 + 16 + 2 + 18 + 14 + 1 = green, **incl. the two override regression
tests** `supplied_cell_widths_override_estimates` and
`raw_rule_supplied_widths_reach_cells`; serializer roundtrip
**12 022/12 022 byte-exact** (the re-sync touched no `dot.rs`/`model.rs`);
`tamarin-server` lib **111** (was 107; the round-10 override + census tests) +
all route suites green.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## B.1 The occupancy adapter — design + where the internal widths come from

**Two sentences.** For every prem/concl record cell the adapter recovers the
reference's *internal* (UN-abbreviated) rendering by fixpoint-expanding the
graph's own legend `NAME = EXPANSION` map back into the display text, then
computes the round-10 shape law on that internal text — `occupancy =
flat_int + Σ_{top-level tuple/union args}(elems+1)`, `fill_width =
flat_int + Σ_{union args}(elems+1) + #function-nodes`, `bonus = max ⌊elems/2⌋+2`
— and supplies it as a per-cell `generate::CellWidths` override through
`group_widths_with` / `RawRule::{premise,conclusion}_widths`. In the live server
these same internal widths are available pre-abbreviation from the ported
pipeline (`graph/abbreviation::compute_abbreviations` keys the `LNTerm` →
alias map, so the un-substituted `pretty_lnterm` width is in scope at the
`handlers/dot` cell-render boundary); the corpus gate recovers them from the
legend because the corpus is post-abbreviation DOT (the legend IS the
open-side view of the internal terms).

The adapter is an OPEN-side measurement harness
(`scratchpad/occ_census`, a standalone crate path-depending on graph-clean); it
computes INPUTS only — no render logic was moved out of the clean module, and
the override for a cell with no abbreviation equals the display-text default
byte-for-byte (so the no-abbreviation cells stay identical to the baseline gate).

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## B.2 GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS gate — adapter ON vs the no-occupancy baseline

Full corpus (12 022 DOT payloads; 615 850 prem/concl cells, 142 540 wrapping,
160 409 records). The harness baseline reproduces the sealed `census.rs`
numbers EXACTLY (same parse/dewrap/`wrap_cell_dot` path), so the only variable
is the override:

    metric (prem/concl)      | BASELINE (no occ) | occ-only        | occ+bonus+fill
    -------------------------|-------------------|-----------------|----------------
    ALL cells byte-exact     | 594789/615850     | 589526/615850   | 589062/615850
                             |   = 96.580 %      |   = 95.726 %    |   = 95.650 %
    wrapping cells (sens.)   | 122957/142540     | 119579/142540   | 119115/142540
                             |   = 86.261 %      |   = 83.892 %    |   = 83.566 %
    multi-cell wrapping      | 111834/131121     | 108456/131121   | 107992/131121
                             |   = 85.291 %      |   = 82.714 %    |   = 82.361 %
    abbreviated-group wrap   |  27818/33217      |  24440/33217    |  23976/33217
                             |   = 83.746 %      |   = 73.577 %    |   = 72.180 %
    (info cells 156464/160409 = 97.541 %; records-all-cells-exact 144092/160409
     = 89.828 % — both unchanged by the override, which touches only prem/concl
     groups.)

**Supplying the internal widths REGRESSES every wrap metric** — most on the very
family it targets (abbreviated groups, −10.2 pp). Per-cell delta (occ-only vs
baseline): **FIXED 372 (117 abbreviated) / BROKEN 5 635 (2 350 abbreviated),
net −5 263.** The lever has a real but tiny correct signal (372 genuine fixes,
the intended non-abbreviated-cell-beside-abbreviated-sibling case) swamped 15× by
breakage. Adding `bonus`+`fill_width` on top of occupancy is marginally worse
still.

**Why (root cause, isolated).** A trigger-only census (does `\l`-presence match,
fill ignored) shows the DISPLAY-flat trigger is already near-perfect and the
internal-width trigger is WORSE:

    trigger accuracy         | display-flat (shipped) | symmetric internal-width
    -------------------------|------------------------|--------------------------
    ALL prem/concl cells     | 613553/615850 99.627 % | 606273/615850 98.445 %
    ABBREVIATED groups       |  65502/65858  99.459 % |  58222/65858  88.405 %

So (i) the shipped size-corrected flat-sum budget already predicts the wrap
DECISION to 99.46 % even on abbreviated groups; charging siblings their full
internal width over-tightens budgets and inflates false-positives
(1 478 → 3 363) — the reference applies a coupled-`fits` RELIEF (a wide sibling
that itself wraps occupies only its ALLOCATED width, not its flat/internal
width), which the raw internal occupancy ignores. (ii) `group_widths_with` uses
the DISPLAY flat for a cell's OWN trigger and routes `occupancy` only into
SIBLINGS' budgets — an asymmetry with no `flat`/self-width override, so it cannot
fix a lone abbreviated cell that wraps on its own internal width (n == 1 returns
87 regardless of override). (iii) even with a correct budget,
`wrap_cell_dot` lays out the POST-abbreviation display text, so an abbreviated
wrapping cell's break POSITIONS (HS lays out the un-abbreviated term, THEN
substitutes names into the broken lines) are unreproducible at ANY budget.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## B.3 Residual families (ranked) — the round-11 targets

Counts from the BASELINE gate (the true target set; the override does not shrink
any of them). First-diverging-byte examples from real corpus records:

1. **NON-abbreviated multi-cell fill divergence — DOMINANT: ~14 954 cells**
   (fillErr 18 764 total − 3 810 abbreviated). Both HS and clean wrap, but break
   at a DIFFERENT element; NO abbreviation involved. This is the fill-ALLOCATION
   gap (clean `group_widths` proportional 87/20 vs HS `renderBalanced 100 (max 30
   . round . (*1.3))` per-field ribbon). The `CellWidths` interface does not
   address it. Example `St_1_gNB( ~gNB_ID, KD1, KD2, '0', AM1, GN2 )` — first
   diverge byte 23: HS breaks after `KD1,` (ragged, on the internal KD/AM/GN
   widths), clean after `KD2,`.
2. **Abbreviated multi-cell fill divergence — ~3 810 cells.** Break positions
   are decided by laying the UN-abbreviated term out then substituting; a
   budget-only override + `wrap_cell_dot(display, b)` cannot reposition them.
   Needs a layout-internal-then-substitute path (a clean-side capability the
   interface does not expose).
3. **False-positive coupled-`fits` relief — 1 478 baseline (→ 3 363 with occ).**
   A wide sibling that wraps frees room for a small cell; internal occupancy
   makes this strictly WORSE, proving the sibling must be charged its ALLOCATED
   (post-wrap) width, not its flat/internal width. Example
   `!Store( ~device, ~handle, ~key, ('1'++n) )` — HS keeps it flat; the
   occupancy override wrongly wraps it (first diverge byte 31) because an
   abbreviated sibling's inflated occupancy shrank this cell's budget.
4. **Lone-abbreviated-cell false-negative — the interface-shaped gap.** A single
   cell that wraps on its OWN internal width (e.g.
   `St_I( ~id, ~ltkA, pk(~ltkB), 'm1', <'commit', pk(~ltkB), pk(~ltkA), ni>, SI4 )`
   — display flat 78, but `SI4` expands past the budget so HS wraps; first
   diverge byte 78). `CellWidths` has no self-width override and `occupancy`
   never enters a cell's own trigger, so it is unclosable through this interface
   as specified.

**Round-11 interface fix (concrete).** For the override to help rather than
regress, the sealed side needs, in priority order: (a) charge a WRAPPING
sibling its allocated width, not its flat/internal width, in the trigger
denominator (models the coupled-`fits` relief that family 3 needs and that the
raw internal occupancy breaks); (b) a per-cell `flat`/self-width override so a
lone cell's OWN trigger can run on the internal width (family 4); (c) a
layout-on-internal-then-substitute cell path so an abbreviated wrapping cell's
break positions match (family 2). Family 1 (the dominant one) is not an
abbreviation problem at all — it is the fill allocator, a clean-side
`group_widths`/`renderBalanced` question independent of `CellWidths`.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## B.4 Adoption — NOT PERFORMED (keep-and-report)

No byte-green adoption surface exists, full or subset:
* The occupancy adapter REGRESSES the corpus (B.2), so routing the live DOT
  serialization through clean `generate` WITH occupancies is strictly worse than
  the ported path, not better.
* Even the no-occupancy baseline reproduces only **89.83 % of records
  all-cells-exact** and **86.26 % of wrapping cells**, so routing through clean
  `generate` (no overrides) still silently regresses ~10 % of records — the
  forbidden class (round-8 B established the ported `render_balanced` is
  byte-faithful to HS on exactly these cells).
* Independently unchanged from rounds 2–8: the clean serializer emits the
  HS-exact dialect (`digraph "G" {`, global `<n_k>` ports, `{{..}|{..}}`) while
  the repo's only byte-sensitive graph test
  (`routes_graph::dot_output_for_a_simple_system`) pins the ported dialect, and
  the captured server graph fixtures are ISE pages (no in-repo byte oracle for a
  dialect switch).

KEPT intact (headers untouched): `handlers/dot.rs` (byte-faithful
`render_balanced` + DotBuilder — 22-author header), `graph/{abbreviation,repr,
simplify,options,render_system}.rs`. `routes_graph` UNCHANGED. `graph_clean` NOT
renamed. Deleted: none. The round-10 override surface is vendored and READY for
the round-11 close once families 1–4 above are addressed clean-side.

--------------------------------------------------------------------------------
## Summary (round-10, unit B) — deleted / kept / header delta

* B.0 RE-SYNCED (`graph_clean/{doclayout,generate,pretty}.rs` round-10,
  headerless; NEW `CellWidths`/`group_widths_with`/`RawRule::*_widths` surface).
  `crate::` → `super::` verified byte-exact; tripwire clean. Serializer roundtrip
  12 022/12 022.
* B.1 Occupancy adapter BUILT (open-side `scratchpad/occ_census`; legend-
  expansion → internal widths → `CellWidths`). Inputs only; no clean logic moved.
* B.2 Corpus gate RUN: baseline all-cells 96.580 % / wrapping 86.261 %; adapter
  ON all-cells 95.726 % / wrapping 83.892 % — a REGRESSION (per-cell FIXED 372 /
  BROKEN 5 635, net −5 263). Root cause isolated: display trigger already
  99.46 % on abbreviated groups; internal occupancy over-charges (missing
  coupled-`fits` relief) + no self-width override + display-text fill layout.
* B.3 Residual re-pinned + ranked (families 1–4) with first-diverging-byte
  examples; concrete round-11 interface fix named.
* B.4 Adoption NOT PERFORMED — adapter regresses + baseline < byte-green +
  dialect/no-oracle. KEPT ported `handlers/dot.rs` + `graph/*`. Deleted: none.

Header-count delta: **133 → 133 (net 0).** No headered file added or deleted; the
three re-synced clean files stayed headerless (tripwire verified). No author
citation disappeared — the swap that would remove `handlers/dot.rs` stays
blocked.

Validation (all green): workspace graph-clean suite (incl. both `CellWidths`
override regression tests); serializer roundtrip 12 022/12 022 byte-exact;
`cargo build -p tamarin-server` 0 errors; `cargo test -p tamarin-server` lib 111
(+2 ignored) + routes (autoprove 6 / basic 19 / graph 4 / proof_step 3 / static 3
/ stubs 15 / upload 3); GRAPHCLEAN_CORPUS occupancy census run over all 12 022
payloads (baseline / occ-only / occ+bonus+fill + trigger-accuracy +
fixed/broken delta).

For every prem/concl record cell the adapter recovers the reference's internal (UN-abbreviated) rendering by fixpoint-expanding the graph's own legend `NAME = EXPANSION` map back into the display text, then computes the round-10 shape law on that internal text (occupancy = flat_int + Σ tuple/union(elems+1); fill_width = flat_int + Σ union(elems+1) + #function-nodes; bonus = max ⌊elems/2⌋+2) and supplies it as a per-cell `generate::CellWidths` override through `group_widths_with` / `RawRule::*_widths`. It is an open-side measurement harness (`/tmp/claude-1000/-home-kamilner-tamarin-rs/57f8e1bc-d495-4b36-93c0-033347d5034a/scratchpad/occ_census/`) that computes INPUTS only — no render logic left the clean module, and a cell with no abbreviation gets the display-text default byte-for-byte.

- ALL prem/concl cells: 594789/615850 = 96.580%  →  occ-only 589526/615850 = 95.726%  (occ+bonus+fill 95.650%)

- Root cause isolated: the shipped display-flat TRIGGER is already 99.627% accurate overall and 99.459% on abbreviated groups; the symmetric internal-width trigger is WORSE (98.445% / 88.405%). Charging siblings their full internal width over-tightens budgets (false-positives 1478→3363, the reference's coupled-`fits` relief), the cell's own trigger has no self-width override (lone-abbreviated false-negatives unclosable), and `wrap_cell_dot` lays out post-abbreviation text so abbreviated cells' break positions are unreproducible at any budget.

Supplying the internal widths REGRESSES the gate; the adapter is therefore NOT usable as-is.

No byte-green surface, full or subset: the occupancy adapter regresses; even the no-override baseline reproduces only 89.828% of records all-cells-exact and 86.261% of wrapping cells (the ported `handlers/dot::render_balanced` is byte-faithful to HS on exactly these, so any route through clean silently regresses ~10% of records); and, unchanged from rounds 2–8, the clean serializer emits the HS dialect (`digraph "G" {`, global `<n_k>` ports) while the only byte-sensitive graph test pins the ported dialect and the repo has no HS graph byte oracle.
